results.possibleIdentity: Wallace Silverman (c. 1920 – 2013) was an electrical design engineer active during the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for his work at the Bunker Ramo Corporation in Stamford, Connecticut. During his career at Bunker Ramo, he played a significant role as a designer of the Nasdaq quotation system, which revolutionized the stock market with automated electronic trading. His technical expertise was in electrical engineering and complex telecommunications devices. Before his time at Bunker Ramo, Silverman served as a World War II veteran in the Army Signal Corps, where he earned two bronze medals for his work in decrypting codes. He also worked for Simon & Schuster's Golden Book Records. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a collaboration-focused non-profit typical for engineers of his caliber in the New York-Connecticut technical corridor during that era. Silverman passed away on December 15, 2013, at the age of 93.
